expectations.text: I imagined it would be like a warm hand on my shoulder. I grew up thinking that when you are a mother, the doctors are there to help you hold everything together.
I expected someone to sit down with me—like a teacher, maybe—and explain things in a way that made me feel calm.
I dreamed of one doctor or midwife—someone who would remember my name without looking at the screen first.

perceptions.text: The people are professional, I suppose, but they are always looking at their computer screens, not at me.
The reality is just... a screen. They point at the gray shadows on the monitor and move very fast.
The first time I heard [the heartbeat], I wanted to cry—not because I was sad, but because it was the only thing in that room that felt true.

valued_items.text: The heartbeat. That is the only time the room gets quiet. The first time I heard it, I wanted to cry.
I just want someone to look at me, truly look at me, and say, 'Adela, you are doing a good job. You are a good mother.'

missing_items.text: I feel like I need someone to translate the meaning, not just the words.
They tell me what I should do, but they never ask what I can do. If they knew I am working full-time... they would know some of their advice... is like asking me to fly.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuous relationship with a known provider.
service_gaps.perception: Seeing a different provider every time; feeling like a jigsaw puzzle.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a primary midwife model or a 'continuity clinic' roster.

service_gaps.expectation: Simple, supportive explanations of health results.
service_gaps.perception: Complex jargon on screens and overwhelming, non-contextual paperwork.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Visual/low-literacy summary reports and 'teach-back' communication protocols.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient Navigator/Community Health Worker role to bridge cultural/emotional gaps and translate clinical instructions into daily life context.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to translate the meaning, not just the words.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual 'safe' support groups to replace the reliance on informal, potentially inaccurate peer advice.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I turn to my community because, even if we are all struggling, we speak the same language of the heart.

innovation_opportunities.description: Personalized 'Life-Plan' intake form that considers socioeconomic constraints (work, rent, childcare) alongside clinical data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They tell me 'eat healthy,' but that is not a plan for a mother who is watching every penny at the grocery store.

expectations.text: I envisioned a partnership where my midwife would see past my professional background and just meet me where I am: exhausted, lonely, and frankly, a little scared.
I expected a sort of threshold moment—a ceremony of sorts where someone would sit down with me, look me in the eye, and say, 'Okay, we’re going to walk this path together'.
I walked into this fully expecting a 'continuity of care' model. That was my ideal—a single, dedicated midwife or small, tight-knit team.

perceptions.text: The reality has been... a strange, polite disconnect. Because of my history in the field, my appointments often feel like a meeting of the minds.
hearing that heartbeat for the first time… that was the only moment the clinical armor cracked. It was a jolt of pure, raw hope that caught me completely off guard.
The reality has been more of a revolving door. I see whoever is on the rotation that week. Sometimes it’s the midwife I’ve met a few times; other times, it’s someone completely new.

valued_items.text: I need someone to just hold space for me, you know? Not as a peer, or a colleague, but as a mom who is doing this mostly by herself.
I need to understand what those results mean for this specific, high-pressure life I’m living.

missing_items.text: It’s that space to just be 'the patient' that’s missing everywhere.
I needed a mirror, not a manual.
No one asked how I was handling the anxiety of the early weeks, or if I needed extra reassurance beyond the standard check-ups.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of a single, dedicated midwife providing holistic care.
service_gaps.perception: A revolving door of providers who lack context of her history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'named midwife' or 'key contact' model for patients with high-anxiety or complex history.

service_gaps.expectation: Clinical interactions that acknowledge her emotional reality and history of loss.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical focus on data and metrics, dismissing vulnerability as 'normal'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate 'emotional status' into EHR alongside vitals; mandatory trauma-informed care training for patient-facing staff.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Human-Centric' digital portal that offers emotional support and reflection, moving away from data-only feedback.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed a mirror, not a manual.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Preconception/Early-Pregnancy Whole-Person Strategy' session that covers social, mental, and physical support systems.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted someone to sit with me and say, 'Okay, you’re doing this largely on your own... Let’s talk about a strategy for your mental health'.

expectations.text: I expected to feel like a mother... I pictured a recovery that was physical, something I could nurse with ice packs and rest.
I thought it would be an expansion of who I already was, like adding a new, beautiful chapter to a book.
I expected those check-ups to be a place to exhale. I thought they would be the moment where someone would finally ask, 'How are you doing, Alessandra?'

perceptions.text: I didn't feel like a mother-to-be. I felt like a patient they were trying to manage before something went wrong.
The support I got felt… clinical. They’d come in, adjust my arms, check the latch, and talk about ounces and intervals.
The appointments felt like efficiency audits. They’d check my incision, look at the charts... and then clear me for 'normal' life.

valued_items.text: I really wanted that closeness, that feeling of finally doing something 'right' after my body had been under such a microscope for months.
I needed someone to look at me—actually look at me—and ask if I was okay.

missing_items.text: There was no space for the fear, no resources for the panic attacks, and definitely no acknowledgment that a twenty-two-year-old alone might need more than a physical exam.
I needed someone to recognize that I was a young woman who was scared to death, not just a feeding vessel.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a partnership focused on humanity and emotional safety.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced transactional, mechanical interactions focused purely on vitals/clinical status.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integration of mental health screening and psychological support as a core metric of clinical success.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected feeding support to be a soft, bonding experience.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced clinical, checklists-based intervention that felt like an intrusion.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Person-centered lactation support that prioritizes maternal psychological state before physical latch mechanics.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of a non-medical 'Maternity Care Advocate' to monitor emotional wellbeing and provide psychological continuity.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would mandate that every high-risk pregnancy... comes with a dedicated, non-medical advocate or therapist integrated into the care team from day one.

innovation_opportunities.description: Holistic 'whole-person' check-up protocols that formally include mental health and trauma-informed assessments as required clinical KPIs.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would tear down the wall that separates 'clinical stability' from 'emotional wellbeing.'

expectations.text: I really thought that if I articulated what I wanted, the medical staff would see me as a partner in the process.
I pictured the hospital as a place where... I’d be treated like a person first and a patient second.
I thought the follow-up care to be a bridge back to 'normal.'... someone to check on me—my stitches, my hormones, but mostly my head.

perceptions.text: My 'plan' didn't matter because the monitors were the only thing they were really listening to.
The reality was clinical, cold, and jarring. As soon as I walked through those double doors, the atmosphere felt sterile and frantic.
The check-ups were short, focused entirely on the babies’ growth charts and my physical healing... there was zero space to talk about the trauma.

valued_items.text: I needed to move, to sway, to find my own rhythm to keep the panic at bay.
I needed someone to sit with me... and just tell me it was okay to be struggling.

missing_items.text: There was never a moment where someone sat down, looked me in the eyes, and said, 'I know you're scared, and I'm right here with you.'
I wanted to feel like a person again, not a medical puzzle.

service_gaps.expectation: To be treated as a partner in the birth process.
service_gaps.perception: Decisions were made for her/to her without consultation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement structured 'shared-decision' visual dashboards that require clinician-patient sign-off on preferences.

service_gaps.expectation: Clinical staff to acknowledge mental and emotional trauma.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive clinical efficiency focused only on physical metrics/data.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated psychological screening tools during postnatal appointments that flag emotional recovery alongside physical checks.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-centric dashboard that visualizes birth preferences, visible to both staff and patient to encourage collaborative care.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I really thought that if I articulated what I wanted, the medical staff would see me as a partner in the process.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postnatal 'Trauma Integration' sessions: mandatory non-clinical check-ins for high-risk birth mothers to process the experience.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I was still stuck in that hospital room, waiting for someone to finally notice me.

innovation_opportunities.description: Mobile monitoring solutions for high-risk patients that allow for greater physical mobility during labour.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I was tethered to the bed by cords and IV lines because of the pre-eclampsia.

expectations.text: I expected those first appointments to be collaborative... a warm space to talk about the transition of adding a third child to my life.
I really expected—perhaps naively—that I would have one, maybe two people who truly knew my history.

perceptions.text: The reality was… jarringly clinical. It wasn’t long before the 'glow' was replaced by that cold, efficient tone.
There is no continuity. Every time I walk in, it’s like starting from scratch with a stranger who barely looks at my file.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to say, 'I know you're scared, and we're going to hold your hand through this'
I wanted to be able to move... gravity and changing positions can help manage the intensity of labor

missing_items.text: What’s missing is the validation that this is hard. No one has ever sat down and just said, 'America, you are carrying a lot'
I have all the 'what' and the 'how,' but I have zero of the 'am I going to be okay?' reassurance.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, warm partner in care
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'case' or 'math problem' to be managed
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a relational care model that prioritizes psychosocial check-ins alongside vitals monitoring.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent care provider who knows history
service_gaps.perception: Rotating cast of strangers requiring history recitation
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Adopt a 'Primary Care Coordinator' or 'Lead Midwife' system for high-risk patients to anchor the clinical file.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-Facing 'Meaning-of-Data' Portal: A digital tool that translates clinical test results into plain-language daily impact assessments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m drowning in data points... What I need is for someone to translate that into how it affects my actual life.

innovation_opportunities.description: Validation-First Nursing Protocol: Incorporate a mandatory 'Emotional Check-in' script during high-risk visits to normalize patient anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What’s missing is the validation that this is hard. No one has ever sat down and just said, 'America, you are carrying a lot.'

expectations.text: I thought there would be this glow, this confidence... someone who would be steady because the love for a child would just drown out any fear.
I expected that maybe someone would walk me through the 'how-to' of being healthy, like how to manage everything without it feeling like a mountain.
I expected the doctor to sit down with me and map it out... help me navigate the 'how' of staying healthy.

perceptions.text: It feels like being a number in a really busy, really crowded office. The nurse was nice, she really was, but she had a list to get through.
Within ten minutes, I wasn't 'Andrea the future mom' anymore. I was just 'Andrea, the medium-risk patient'.
They were so focused on the next pregnancy and the clinical risks that my past loss felt completely invisible.

valued_items.text: The positive side of it was just realizing that someone did care enough to mention the folic acid, that at least there's a protocol to keep a baby safe.
A little bit of empathy would have gone a long way, like acknowledging that it’s hard to prioritize 'fresh, healthy food'.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to talk to, someone who could act as a bridge between the clinical world and the real, messy, lonely world I’m living in.
Nobody asked how I was handling the anxiety of starting over... There wasn't a counselor, a support group.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized guidance on how to manage health amidst life's constraints.
service_gaps.perception: Standardized clinical checklists with no regard for socioeconomic barriers.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop patient-centered care planning tools that integrate social determinants of health.

service_gaps.expectation: Emotional acknowledgment of personal history and grief.
service_gaps.perception: Cold, purely clinical focus on 'at risk' status.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement routine trauma-informed intake screening for prior loss and social isolation.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Bridge Support' program connecting patients with peer mentors who share similar lived experiences (e.g., solo motherhood, bereavement).
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to talk to, someone who could act as a bridge between the clinical world and the real, messy, lonely world I’m living in.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Life-Context' care planning tool that maps medical goals to individual transit and financial realities.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wish someone had just asked me, 'Andrea, what do you need to make this possible?' not just 'what do you need to do to be healthy?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Curate realistic, inclusive digital content libraries that avoid the 'Pinterest-perfect' aesthetic and focus on practical, low-budget advice.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m looking for someone to say, 'I know you're tired, and here is how you manage your health when life is hard.'

expectations.text: I was hoping for a bit more of a partnership... someone to sit down with me, look at the whole picture—not just the blood pressure or the weight—and say, 'Okay, Alyssa, we know money is tight... let’s figure out a way to get you through this safely.'
I expected that if I was labeled 'high risk,' there would be some kind of direct line.
I think the biggest dream I had was that I’d have one person—one face I could recognize—who would actually remember my name without looking at my chart.

perceptions.text: It’s hurry up and wait in the lobby, then quick-fire questions in a sterile room that smells like antiseptic. They run the tests, they frown at the numbers, and then they give me these pamphlets... It’s cold, honestly.
It’s a revolving door. Every appointment feels like a fresh start with a stranger. One time I see a midwife, the next it’s a nurse practitioner, and then maybe a doctor I’ve never spoken to before.
It’s all on me to execute this perfect plan... They tell me the what—like 'keep your blood pressure down'—but they don't give me the how that fits into a life like mine.

valued_items.text: I’ve had a neighbor who occasionally drops off a bag of apples or some fresh greens when she hits a sale—that was a surprise, honestly. She doesn’t ask questions, she just leaves them on the porch.
I just want someone to look at me, truly look at me, and validate how much I’m carrying.

missing_items.text: I don't need another pamphlet; I need someone to listen to why I'm struggling to get those specific foods.
I need to know why my blood pressure is doing what it's doing in a way that doesn't just make me feel like I’m a broken machine.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, holistic care planning that accounts for socio-economic reality.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, cookie-cutter clinical warnings that ignore the patient's lived context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create 'Context-Aware Care Plans' that allow patients to document socio-economic stressors.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, named provider relationship.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of unfamiliar staff leading to emotional exhaustion and lack of trust.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a primary midwife/lead-professional model with a digital 'passport' for information.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Real-Life Nutrition' bridge tool that maps medical nutritional requirements to low-cost grocery flyers or food bank resources.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They give me these clinical warnings, but they never bridge the gap between 'you need better nutrition' and the reality of my kitchen at home.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'High-Risk Rapid Response' digital channel that avoids phone trees and provides immediate triage with a known contact.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I expected that if I was labeled 'high risk,' there would be some kind of direct line.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Pre-Visit Context Form' in the patient portal to share life stressors/questions before the consult to save time and build rapport.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I spend the first ten minutes of every single visit playing catch-up, explaining my medical history while they frantically flip through my file.

expectations.text: I imagined walking into an office and having someone see me—not just the chart, not just my age—and really guiding me through the transition of becoming a mother.
I thought I’d have a team around me.
I thought I’d find straightforward answers that would make me feel like I was checking off a list of 'to-dos'.

perceptions.text: Everything is fast. I go in, I pee in a cup, I get weighed, and they give me these glossy pamphlets that seem written for someone else... It feels less like care and more like a transaction.
I saw the nurse’s eyes shift toward my file when she saw there was no partner listed. That silence in the room suddenly felt heavy, like she was silently assessing my capability.
I never see the same person twice... Every single time I go into that clinic, I have to re-explain who I am.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to say, 'If you feel this, call us,' or 'Here is someone you can talk to if you're feeling scared about a potential loss.'
I wanted that continuity because I figured that if they knew me, they’d know when I was scared or when I was just being a first-time mom.

missing_items.text: There was no warmth, no acknowledgement that I was scared, and certainly no one asking if I had support at home.
I feel like there’s this huge, empty space in the middle where a real conversation about being scared and nineteen is supposed to happen.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, mentor-like guidance through pregnancy
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, fast-paced clinical visits focused on data points
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a primary midwife model for continuity.

service_gaps.expectation: Support for mental/emotional well-being during hormonal changes
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive responses attributing mental state solely to hormones
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate perinatal mental health screening and support access.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital 'peer-mentor' platform specifically for young, single mothers to avoid the 'perfection' bias of standard forums.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m looking for someone to say, 'Hey, it’s okay to be young and scared,' but instead, I just find scary statistics or happy, shiny advice.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'warm-line' triage service that prioritizes emotional reassurance over purely clinical risk-assessment for low-risk patients.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They always ask, 'Is it an emergency?' in this tone that makes it clear they hope I say 'no.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Electronic health record 'Patient Narrative' flag that alerts providers to psychosocial context (e.g., young, single, isolated) before the appointment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I have to re-explain who I am, why I’m there, and that I’m nineteen and on my own.

expectations.text: I expected to be tired, sure, but I guess I thought I’d be this empowered new mom, adjusting easily.
I thought the doctors would be looking for the cracks in my armor—not just checking my incision or my blood pressure, but asking if the walls were closing in on me.
I expected the maternity care system to be like a lighthouse—something that would shine a beam of safety onto the path ahead.

perceptions.text: The nurses would come in and give me these quick demonstrations—here’s how to latch, here’s how to position—but it was so clinical.

They treated me like a diagnosis, not a woman. They were excellent at monitoring my blood pressure, my weight, and my physical progress, but they were completely blind to the woman who was driving herself home.

valued_items.text: I needed someone to sit with me long enough to see the panic in my eyes. I needed someone to acknowledge that doing this by myself... is heavy.
It helps to be heard.

missing_items.text: I just wish they had asked me how I was feeling, instead of just checking if I was doing 'well.'
I needed someone to say, 'I know you're tired, and I know it's hard to be the only one awake right now.'

service_gaps.expectation: Expectation of a 'lighthouse' support system for an unsupported, solo parent.
service_gaps.perception: Transactional, 'check-the-box' clinical interactions focused solely on physical stats.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal follow-ups to include standardized social determinants of health screening and holistic well-being assessments.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected professionals to identify emotional 'cracks in the armor' beyond physical recovery.
service_gaps.perception: Professional dismissal of mental health concerns as 'just an adjustment'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Trauma-Informed' communication training and mental health red-flag screening for providers.

innovation_opportunities.description: Social Risk Triage Tool: Redefine 'Risk' to include social isolation, living arrangements, and lack of support, not just medical indicators.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: being 'low risk' physically doesn't mean you aren't 'high risk' emotionally or socially.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual Village/Peer-Support Integration: A digital bridge to connect solo parents with community resources and peer emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m essentially grieving the support I don’t have, but I’m doing it in silence.

innovation_opportunities.description: Extended Holistic Postpartum Consults: Transition from 10-minute physical audits to 30-minute wellbeing-centered postpartum check-ups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They gave me a clean bill of health, but I still felt like I was drowning in that house.

expectations.text: I truly believed that because I knew the terminology, I’d be treated as a partner in my own care.
I expected to be the woman who managed with breathing techniques, visualization, and maybe a little nitrous if things got hairy.
I thought that familiarity would act like a warm blanket, a signal to my nervous system that I was in a place of safety and expertise.

perceptions.text: They’re treating me like a colleague, asking me to weigh in on the 'plan' while I’m trying to survive the next wave of pain.
I didn't feel like I was *with* them in the decision; I felt like I was being processed by a machine I helped build.
The lactation consultants were wonderful, but they approached me with the same 'colleague-to-colleague' intensity.

valued_items.text: I just want my doctor to hold my hand and tell me, 'Alica, everything is going to be fine'
I wanted to be treated like a scared woman who just needed permission to let go.

missing_items.text: I wanted the option to be a patient who is simply cared for. I wanted to be able to say, 'I am overwhelmed, please help me,' and have that request met with a gentle hand
She didn't ask how I felt about the intervention; she just started citing the fetal heart rate variability and the risk of uterine fatigue.

service_gaps.expectation: Partnered decision making based on shared knowledge.
service_gaps.perception: Consultation-heavy interactions that bypass emotional needs and place the burden of choice on the patient.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop 'Non-Clinical Patient Pathways' for staff who are patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Sanctuary-like environment providing comfort and safety.
service_gaps.perception: Aggressively clinical, cold, and hyper-exposed 'fishbowl' environment.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create 'Sanctuary Protocols' to mask clinical sterility for staff-patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Humanity-First' bypass code or flag for healthcare staff patients to signal that they want to 'leave their coat at the door' and receive standard, non-clinical supportive care.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want an epidural, but I want it without a consultation. I want it without being treated like a peer.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postnatal support groups specifically for healthcare professionals to address 'Clinical Brain' anxiety and the loss of professional identity during motherhood.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wasn't asking for a diagnosis; I was asking for someone to hold space for the fact that my professional knowledge was sabotaging my transition to motherhood.

expectations.text: I thought it would be about celebrating, about talking through what we learned last time, and having a team that would really hold my hand.
I pictured having that one midwife or doctor I could call, someone who knew my kids’ names or remembered how terrified I was the last time around.
I thought a birth plan was supposed to be my voice when I couldn't speak for myself.

perceptions.text: It’s cold. It’s all fluorescent lights and those sterile, paper-lined tables... The appointments are rushed.
I feel like I’m constantly re-introducing myself to a new face in a white coat. Every time I show up, it’s whoever is on the rotation that day.
The moment things started to get shaky, that piece of paper might as well have been a grocery list for all the notice it got.

valued_items.text: I wanted to be treated like a person, not a textbook case.
I need someone to talk to me about what I can realistically do to lower my risk without feeling like I’m failing as a mother.

missing_items.text: Everything feels like it’s missing, but if I had to pick, it’s the human element.
No one talks to me about resources... or how to manage nutrition when money is tight.

service_gaps.expectation: Access to a consistent provider who knows my history and family context.
service_gaps.perception: A revolving door of strangers who require me to recite my trauma every visit.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Dedicated high-risk care navigation/case management

service_gaps.expectation: Birth plan as a tool for autonomy and communication of preferences.
service_gaps.perception: Plan ignored during intervention; mother felt like cargo being moved through a procedure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital birth-preference integration into EHR with mandatory clinical review

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Known-Caregiver' model or digital continuity profile to prevent re-traumatizing patients through repetitive history-taking.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I have to retell my story over and over—the eclampsia, the seizures, the fear—as if it’s just a script I’m reciting for a stranger.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated 'Life-Context' screening to address non-clinical barriers (finances, childcare) alongside medical risk.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They treat my life circumstances like they're outside their jurisdiction. If I’m a 'high-risk patient,' then my life—my actual, everyday life—is part of that risk.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-Facing 'Clinical Translator' digital tool that explains medical data and terminology in plain, human-centered language.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need them to explain the 'what if' in a way that’s human.

expectations.text: I imagined that when I walked into the office, we’d skip the basic brochures and go straight to the nuanced, high-level stuff.
I wanted a space to talk through the 'what-ifs' with someone who wouldn't just dismiss them as 'not a problem yet.'

perceptions.text: The system is just not built for that level of preventative mental space. When I brought it up, the response was very much, 'You're healthy, everything looks normal, just start trying and let us know.'

It’s been frustrating. I either get the dumbed-down version that leaves me feeling like they’re hiding the risks, or I fall down a rabbit hole of medical literature that just spikes my cortisol levels.

valued_items.text: I desperately need someone to look past the medical jargon and just ask, 'How are you doing with this weight?'

missing_items.text: I need someone to tell me it’s okay to put down the stethoscope and just be a woman who is trying to bring a life into the world.
They don't have a toggle setting for 'I’m a clinician, skip the fluff and show me the data.'

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, high-level clinical conversation acknowledging professional background.
service_gaps.perception: Standard 'low-risk' protocol and dismissive 'you're fine' rhetoric.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop clinical-tier risk assessment tools for medical professionals in maternity care.

service_gaps.expectation: Proactive mental health care planning for anxious patients.
service_gaps.perception: System is only reactive to pathology/actual complications.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Pre-conception mental health screening and care mapping as a standard service.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Clinician Mode' for pregnancy apps that filters out 'fluff' content and provides high-fidelity, peer-reviewed clinical data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They don't have a toggle setting for 'I’m a clinician, skip the fluff and show me the data.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a specialized 'Medical Professional Consultation' pathway where OBs are trained to pivot from clinical advice to peer-support dialogue.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to move toward a version of care where the provider sees the person behind the chart—where they might look at me and say, 'I know you know the stats, but let's talk about the feeling of this.'

expectations.text: I think I expected someone to sit down with me, hold my hand, and maybe say, 'Brandi, it’s going to be okay, we’ve got a plan for you.'
I had this vision of a 'primary' person—someone who would know my name before they looked at my chart... who held the thread of my whole story.
I thought a doctor sits down, pulls up a chair, and explains things in a way that actually makes sense.

perceptions.text: The reality? It’s just... clinical. It’s cold. You walk into that sterile room, and it’s like I’m already on an assembly line.
The reality is a frustrating maze of automated menus and 'leave a message' voicemails. When I’m sitting in my house in Leicester... that silence on the other end of the line is deafening.
They’re efficient, don't get me wrong. They’re doing their jobs.

valued_items.text: I wanted a partner in this, someone to help me feel brave enough to do it again.
I needed someone to say, 'Brandi, I know you’re terrified, and I know this is a long, lonely drive, so let’s talk about how we can make this work for you.'

missing_items.text: There wasn't a single question about how I was coping at home or if I had any support.
They give me these instructions, but they don't give me the tools or the time to talk about why I’m struggling to follow them.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistency in care provider to know history and story.
service_gaps.perception: A revolving door of different providers requiring repeated traumatic history disclosure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient summary dashboard or assigned primary midwife lead.

service_gaps.expectation: Accessible, responsive system for urgent concerns.
service_gaps.perception: Automated, slow, and non-responsive gatekeeping via voicemails.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: 24/7 high-risk direct triage line/mobile app with real-time specialist chat.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'High-Risk Primary Advocate' program where one assigned midwife manages the personal-care plan while specialists manage medical data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I had this vision of a 'primary' person—someone who would know my name before they looked at my chart.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Patient-Led Care Briefing' tool that allows patients to upload or highlight key emotional/social concerns that must be addressed before clinical data review.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It wasn't even a conversation; it was a process of checking boxes... skipping over 'How are you feeling, Brandi?' to dive straight into the medical protocols.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual home-monitoring platform for high-risk rural patients to reduce travel burden and provide 'asynchronous' support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m expected to manage these terrifying health markers in the middle of nowhere... the drive is a struggle.

expectations.text: I imagined myself walking into the clinic, confident and informed... I thought the medical support would feel like a partnership of equals.
I expected a clear, coordinated plan that treated my health and my pregnancy as one cohesive story.
I expected a system that functioned like a safety net—someone I could call, or a quick portal message that would get a thoughtful, nuanced response.

perceptions.text: The support I’m getting is excellent technically—my midwife is truly wonderful—but the 'support' I actually feel is totally different from what I expected.
It’s not just a clinical appointment; it’s an emotional gauntlet... the appointments felt chopped into fifteen-minute blocks.
My results arrived in a patient portal—that sterile, blue-tinted interface—usually late in the evening... devoid of context, tone, or warmth.

valued_items.text: The moment that 'medium risk' tag appeared on my chart, that sense of control I valued so much just evaporated.
The only thing that felt real and unscripted... was that moment of grace when we found the heartbeat.

missing_items.text: I need a space where I can stop being the expert and just be someone who is, frankly, pretty scared and overwhelmed.
What’s truly missing is the human element—the bridge between the data and the person.

service_gaps.expectation: Coordinated, proactive, relationship-based care.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented, reactive care where the patient must self-manage and bridge hand-offs.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Dedicated patient navigator or case manager for high-anxiety/high-risk profiles.

service_gaps.expectation: A partnership of equals based on shared medical literacy.
service_gaps.perception: The system forces a rigid professional/patient hierarchy, dismissing patient concerns due to her background.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider training on 'biopsychosocial' intake assessments that bypass standard 'new patient' scripts for high-literacy patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Human-Centric Digital Concierge' that offers asynchronous but nuanced, high-level clinical reassurance for patient questions, moving away from binary 'safe/emergency' triage.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need a mentor, not a data entry clerk.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Primary Midwife Continuity' model for patients identified as medium-risk, ensuring a single point of emotional and clinical contact.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted a partner who would know my face, my history, and my fears without having to scroll through a tablet to find them.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a facilitated peer-support group for high-literacy/professional pregnant women to mitigate the isolation of having to suppress vulnerabilities.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need a space where I can stop being the expert and just be someone who is, frankly, pretty scared and overwhelmed.

expectations.text: I expected the physical recovery to be about healing a wound... I thought I’d be the resilient mother who handles the transition with grace.
I expected those check-ups to be a safe harbor. I thought they would be the one place where I could set down the 'nurse' armor.

perceptions.text: The visits felt like a transaction—a clinical checklist being ticked off.
I felt this immense, crushing pressure to keep the mask on, to report back with the poise of a professional.

valued_items.text: I wanted to talk to someone, to have someone else hold her for a bit so I could just close my eyes.

missing_items.text: The system should be designed to bridge that gap between the office visit and the 3:00 AM panic.
I needed them to ask, 'How are you holding up?' and actually wait for the messy, unvarnished answer.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected care providers to see past credentials to the human needs underneath.
service_gaps.perception: Providers focused on professional rapport and checklist-based clinical diagnostics.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement clinician-patient 'de-masking' training for providers dealing with healthcare-professional patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a peer-support or specialized support line for healthcare workers who are new mothers to provide a safe space outside of the formal medical chart.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We need to normalize the fact that 'resilient' mothers are still terrified.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrate 'human check-ins' into postnatal visits that are explicitly decoupled from physical vitals and clinical checklists.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The system needs to stop viewing a mother as a 'medium risk' on a spreadsheet and start treating her as a person in a specific life context.

expectations.text: I had this vision of sitting down with one of the midwives and going through it, point by point, like it was a shield I could carry into the room with me.
I wanted it to feel like a sanctuary. I hoped for a room that didn't feel so sterile—maybe some dim light, somewhere to put my bag where I didn't feel like a stranger or a patient number.
I thought the conversation about pain would be a real partnership. I imagined the midwives sitting with me, laying out the pros and cons, and asking me what I felt I could handle.

perceptions.text: Once the contractions really hit, the staff stopped looking at the plan and started looking at the monitors. The 'how' of my birth plan vanished in the rush of the hospital routine.
The lights were that harsh, buzzing kind that make my skin crawl... It was just 'get into this gown, get into this bed, let’s hook you up to the machines.'
The follow-up felt like a conveyor belt. The appointments were short, focused entirely on the baby’s weight... It was like they were looking at a spreadsheet, not a person.

valued_items.text: I just want someone to listen to me, not just my vitals. I need to know I’m going to be treated like a person.
I wanted someone to help me figure out how to navigate [pain] without feeling like I was just 'weak' for wanting relief or 'stubborn' for trying to avoid it.

missing_items.text: I really wanted more than just the binary choice of 'tough it out' or 'numb everything.' I had read about water therapy.
I wanted emotional support that acknowledged that this wasn't just a clinical process, but a massive life event for a nineteen-year-old.

service_gaps.expectation: Midwife to sit down and review birth plan point-by-point to ensure agency.
service_gaps.perception: Plan ignored or treated as a menu; staff focused on monitor protocols.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital birth plan integration into EHR that triggers mandatory review prompts.

service_gaps.expectation: Environmental adaptation for safety and comfort (lighting, personal space).
service_gaps.perception: Harsh, clinical, sterile environment prioritized over patient psychological comfort.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Modular room design with adjustable sensory controls for trauma-informed care.

service_gaps.expectation: Holistic postnatal check-up focused on maternal mental health and trauma recovery.
service_gaps.perception: Conveyor-belt approach focused solely on infant weight and clinical metrics.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized trauma-informed screening tools and extended mental health triage appointments.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-Informed Care Training (TICT) for clinical staff to recognize signs of dissociation and validate patient emotional state.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed a partner, someone to bridge the gap between my trauma and the intensity of birth, but all I got were clinicians who were just waiting to get to the end of the shift.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of a 'Golden Hour' protocol that prioritizes skin-to-skin contact before routine clinical measurements.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: My baby wasn't placed on me; they were whisked away to some station for checks I didn't even understand.

innovation_opportunities.description: Creation of 'Trauma-Informed Postnatal Support' pathways that include dedicated social support and mental health counseling alongside standard check-ups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They give you these pamphlets on 'self-care' and 'healing,' but those sheets don’t account for the fact that my body feels like a crime scene.

expectations.text: I expected a sense of safety... a calm, supportive circle where you’re guided, nurtured, and held.
I expected a partnership... someone who understood the specific cocktail of fear and history I bring into that room.
I envisioned a sit-down—a moment where someone would pull up a chair, look me in the eye, and translate the data into what it actually meant for my life and my baby.

perceptions.text: It’s cold. It’s transactional. Because I’m forty-one and my blood pressure has been creeping up, the 'gentle' part of midwifery care vanished.
They treat me like a colleague, which just means they don't treat me like a scared patient.
It’s a revolving door. I rarely see the same person twice.

valued_items.text: I wanted an advocate, but I’m just getting a medical team who assumes I can manage my own crisis.
What I need is someone to acknowledge that I am a woman who is scared to death... who feels like she's waiting for a bomb to go off inside her.

missing_items.text: They give me the numbers, but they never give me the care.
There is no one to help me navigate the logistical nightmare of working (or trying to) while my body is constantly on the verge of a medical emergency.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional partnership and collaborative decision-making regarding high-risk management.
service_gaps.perception: Isolation, dismissal, and being treated as a 'high-risk case' rather than a patient.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a 'Patient-Led Care Plan' tool that forces clinicians to reconcile the patient's narrative with clinical protocol.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care to foster trust and reduce the burden of repeating history.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care through a revolving door of providers requiring the patient to re-perform her history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Primary Midwife' model specifically for high-risk patients to anchor the clinical team.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'High-Risk Patient Navigator' role to bridge the gap between clinical data and emotional/logistical support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They give me the numbers, but they never give me the care.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Patient-Centric Portal' where the patient's personal concerns are documented alongside medical vitals, ensuring providers review the 'whole person' before appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They don't seem to read the chart until they’re sitting in front of me.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement 'Emotional Triage' training for maternity staff to prevent the dehumanization of patients with medical backgrounds.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: My background became an excuse for them to withhold the very thing I needed most: basic human empathy.

expectations.text: I imagined my preconception care would be a collaborative, data-driven experience.
I thought I’d find tools that allowed me to input my own data... and get back something personalized.

perceptions.text: The appointments feel like a rapid-fire checklist. When I walk in, I see the doctor glance at my file, see '44,' see 'single,' and I can almost watch her switch into a simplified mode of communication.
I’m treated like I’m just starting to learn how human reproduction works. It makes me feel incredibly isolated.

valued_items.text: I wanted a partner who can hold the complexity with me—someone who acknowledges that yes, the biology is daunting.
I wanted a plan that felt as precise as the research I conduct daily.

missing_items.text: No one is telling me, 'Here is the biological truth of your situation, here are the limitations, and here is how we walk that path together with intelligence.'
The system isn't set up to 'prepare' a woman of forty-four; it’s set up to 'monitor' her for failure.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, data-driven partnership with clinician.
service_gaps.perception: Reductive, rapid-fire checklist and dismissal of intellectual inquiry.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement clinician-led 'data-informed' consultation protocols for high-health-literacy patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, evidence-based digital health tools.
service_gaps.perception: Patronizing baby-speak and marketing-driven generic advice.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop clinical-grade preconception dashboards that integrate personal health markers and longitudinal research.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'high-literacy' or 'science-partner' care track that allows patients to review research and metabolic data with clinicians instead of standard protocols.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We need a maternity care model that stops treating the patient’s intelligence as a hurdle.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a clinical data synthesis tool that translates current research on maternal age and stress markers into a personalized, non-anecdotal care plan.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m looking for a nuanced discussion about biological aging, but the internet wants to give me generic platitudes.

expectations.text: I pictured someone pulling up a chair, looking me in the eye, and maybe acknowledging that this was a big, scary thing to be doing by myself.
I thought I’d have one person who knew I was doing this on my own, who knew that my support system is practically non-existent.
I expected them to anticipate my questions—or at least leave space for them—rather than just rushing to the next patient.

perceptions.text: The reality was a lot more sterile. The office here in Holden is clean and efficient, but it’s fast.
Everything shows up on this portal app on my phone before I even get a call... I’m left to piece together the health of my own child through an app.
The reality, though, is that I see whoever is on rotation. It’s like a revolving door.

valued_items.text: Seeing that little flicker on the screen for the first time... I didn't feel like a data point anymore. I felt like a mother.
They don’t see me as a “risk factor” or a “statistical concern.” They see me as someone who is scared.

missing_items.text: I just wish someone would ask me how I’m *doing*, not just how my vitals are.
I’ve never once had someone ask me, “What are your preferences for the birth?”
I’m not asking for a friendship, just a little bit of recognition.

service_gaps.expectation: Relationship-based care with a known provider who understands personal history.
service_gaps.perception: Rotating staff who treat patient as a stranger and check charts without context.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a primary midwife model or 'named-provider' system for vulnerable/single patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Clinical communication that bridges the gap between lab reports and personal well-being.
service_gaps.perception: Automated app notifications and 'textbook' summaries that ignore patient anxiety.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign patient portal to include 'Personalized Care Briefs' that interpret data in the context of the patient's specific life situation.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Single Mother Peer Support' program facilitated by the clinic to bridge the gap in community support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I joined these forums for expectant mothers, and there are women there... who are the only ones who truly get it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Patient Holistic Intake' tool that captures social and emotional context to prevent the 'data point' feeling.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If they knew how much I struggle with the isolation... they might approach things differently.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Consent & Context' protocol for physical examinations to prevent feelings of violation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: That lack of basic consideration—just asking for permission or explaining the touch—it made me feel so small.

expectations.text: I imagined a partnership... A provider who would sit with me, acknowledge the trauma... and help me build a plan that didn't just revolve around monitoring me like I’m a piece of malfunctioning equipment.
I expected some form of 'trauma-informed' care... someone sitting down and acknowledging that my body had been through a catastrophe.
I imagined a partner who would be the one to ask the doctors, 'How is she feeling? What can we do to make this easier on her?'

perceptions.text: The care is so segmented. I show up... and the provider barely has time to look up from their screen. They see 'Eclampsia, G1'... and their whole demeanor shifts.
I was shocked by how quickly my voice was erased. I felt like I was being told that because my body failed me once, I had lost the right to have a say.
They act like every question I ask... is a sign that I’m being 'difficult' or 'non-compliant.'

valued_items.text: I wanted a guide who respected my body's potential, not just its capacity to fail.
It’s the simple absence of a witness... I need someone to say, 'I know you're terrified, and that makes sense.'

missing_items.text: I don't have a support network to call... It’s not just about needing a ride... it’s about needing someone to remind me that I’m more than just a pregnancy that might go wrong.
I wanted the context. I need them to explain the trend lines... to tell me what I can actually do to help stabilize things.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized partnership for managing medical history
service_gaps.perception: Clinical policing and liability-focused interaction
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Co-designed care plans that integrate patient research

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity with a dedicated provider
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of clinicians with no prior knowledge of case
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Dedicated case-management/midwifery team assignments

service_gaps.expectation: Support for social/logistical barriers
service_gaps.perception: Complete absence of support for transport and isolation
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated social-work support and mobile/virtual clinic options

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual care integration for stable patients to reduce physical transit burden and associated stress/blood pressure spikes.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Getting to the clinic in West Boylston without a car feels like a mountain... I wanted to see if we could consolidate appointments or find a way to minimize the stress.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed patient dashboard that summarizes historical trauma and preferences, ensuring any clinician sees the 'whole person' first.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They see 'Eclampsia, G1' on the intake form, and their whole demeanor shifts... There’s no space for the fear I feel.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer-support/Mentor matching program for high-risk, isolated pregnancies to provide 'a witness'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It’s the simple absence of a witness... I don't have a support network to call.

expectations.text: I thought if I understood the mechanics... I’d be in control.
I thought feeding would be the one thing I could truly own... a quiet, bonding anchor.
I expected those check-ups to be a space for decompression... a partnership.

perceptions.text: They’d use these simplified, patronizing tones, like I was some naive kid.
My appointments felt like a quick assembly line... they were managing the symptoms of a machine.
The medical community seems to view the 'patient' as someone who doesn't know their own biology.

valued_items.text: I needed them to see me—not just as a 'case'... but as a person who was terrified.
I needed a partner who would help me process the medical trauma.

missing_items.text: They utterly failed to manage me. They didn't see a researcher with agency; they saw a young, single woman to be managed.
I would build a system that acknowledges that a high-risk birth is a major life-altering event... integrated psychological support.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner-style collaboration where her intellect was used as a tool.
service_gaps.perception: Patronizing, assembly-line treatment where her knowledge was dismissed as a character flaw.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider communication training for 'expert patients'.

service_gaps.expectation: Check-ups as a space for psychological and emotional decompression.
service_gaps.perception: Mechanical, metric-driven 'case closure' without inquiry into mental health.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Trauma-informed care pathways for high-risk postpartum recovery.

innovation_opportunities.description: Continuity of personhood model using dedicated, trauma-informed care coordinators for high-risk patients for at least 6 months postpartum.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would move away from this assembly-line check-up structure and replace it with a model that assigns a dedicated, trauma-informed care coordinator.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of mental health screening that accounts for medical trauma and 'expert' anxiety, moving beyond basic depression checklists.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They’d check off a box on a depression screening form... but that form doesn't capture the nuance of someone who knows too much.

expectations.text: I really hoped that if I presented it [birth plan] professionally, they’d see me as someone who deserved a voice, not just a 'never married' statistic.
I pictured a supportive environment where I’d be encouraged to find my own rhythm, where I could decide how much pain I wanted to lean into.
I wanted her skin against mine immediately, feeling her warmth to ground me after the storm of the birth.

perceptions.text: They barely glanced at it [birth plan]. They started talking over me almost immediately, using those cold, clinical terms.
They treated my body like it was this fragile, ticking bomb that couldn't be trusted to stand up or move.
The atmosphere felt incredibly cold and transactional... faces would instantly drop into this practiced, robotic mask.

valued_items.text: I wanted to feel like I had a seat at the table, like I was a partner in my own care.
I wanted someone to just say, 'You're doing okay, Angelyn.'

missing_items.text: I felt like I was being robbed of the experience of my own child’s birth.
I wanted help—some kind of support, a therapist, just a person who would hear the trauma.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner in care / Voice in birth plan
service_gaps.perception: Objectified as a medical 'risk' or statistic; input ignored
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement digital birth plan platform that mandates clinician acknowledgment and response

service_gaps.expectation: Compassionate, human-centered emotional support
service_gaps.perception: Cold, clinical, robotic, and transactional interaction
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated doula or patient-advocate program for 'medium-risk' pregnancies

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-facing digital dashboard that tracks birth plan preferences and clinician acknowledgments to ensure patient voice is not overridden without formal discussion.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They barely glanced at it [birth plan]... it was a surrender.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed care pathways for postnatal appointments that include mandatory mental health screening and dedicated space for psychological recovery, not just vitals monitoring.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The doctor didn't even look up from the chart... she just asked me if I was taking my blood pressure medication.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implementation of 'Communication Coaches' or sensitivity training to address implicit bias against young, unmarried, or multiracial mothers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m a young, multiracial woman, unmarried... I can see the way their eyes glaze over when I try to describe my pain.

expectations.text: I imagined walking into the clinic and being welcomed into this sort of sisterhood of medicine—where the doctors would treat me like a colleague in the making.
I expected a "continuity of care" model that mirrored what I studied in my clinical rotations—the idea of a dedicated provider who actually knows the story behind the chart.
I expected the results to be a data-driven partnership... a conversation between two people who respected the data.

perceptions.text: The reality? It felt… clinical. Cold. When I walked in for that first appointment, I didn’t feel like a medical student; I felt like a box to be checked.
The reality, however, has been a rotating door of providers... I’ve never once felt like a person to them—just a set of vitals.
The nurse—not even the doctor—just stopped. She didn't look at the chart... She put her hand on my arm and said, 'Anja, it is okay to be scared. You are doing something incredibly brave.'

valued_items.text: She put her hand on my arm and said, 'Anja, it is okay to be scared. You are doing something incredibly brave.'
I want to be treated like an adult who could handle the weight of the information.

missing_items.text: Everything feels so transactional... I just needed them to acknowledge that my context matters.
There was no 'Is this a good time?' or 'Are you ready?' It was just a command.

service_gaps.expectation: Partnership based on medical knowledge/collegiality.
service_gaps.perception: Infantilizing, 'mom-to-be' scripts and patronizing communication.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Personalized patient onboarding that identifies professional backgrounds/literacy levels to calibrate communication style.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care with a dedicated provider.
service_gaps.perception: Rotating door of providers requiring repetitive, dehumanizing explanations of her situation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Dedicated 'Patient Advocate' or 'Primary Midwife' model for patients without traditional social support systems.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Context-Aware' medical record feature where patient social/personal context is prioritized alongside clinical data, preventing the 'cold clinical' approach.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I just needed them to acknowledge that my context matters.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Validated Patient' protocol that replaces infantilizing 'cheerleader' scripts with evidence-based, data-driven communication for highly literate or medically-trained patients.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I didn't want the 'happy-talk' pamphlet; I wanted the medical reality.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Standardized Informed Consent' module for pelvic exams that requires verbal confirmation and explicit emotional check-ins, moving away from 'doing to' the patient.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There was no 'Is this a good time?' or 'Are you ready?' It was just a command.

expectations.text: I thought I’d feel like a partner in my own health, not just a patient file that needs to be managed.
I expected a collaborative plan. I wanted a provider to map out a clear path for me.

perceptions.text: They see me and immediately shift into 'shorthand mode,' rattling off iron counts and titer levels like I’m a colleague rather than a scared woman.
It feels like they’re trying to insulate themselves against the possibility of a bad outcome, which makes me feel like a liability.

valued_items.text: I need someone to bridge that gap between the clinical risks and the fact that I’m just a terrified twenty-three-year-old girl.
I wanted a provider to look at me—not my chart—and ask, 'How are you really doing?'

missing_items.text: Not having a partner means there’s no one to share the small, quiet moments of fear with.
I expected to find community—you know, forums or apps where women shared the real, messy, vulnerable parts of trying to conceive after a loss.

service_gaps.expectation: Comforting, personalized counseling acknowledging medical history and emotional fear.
service_gaps.perception: Cold, clinical, jargon-heavy interactions that dismiss emotional state in favor of laboratory results.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementation of trauma-informed care protocols that mandate a 'human connection' step before discussing clinical diagnostics.

service_gaps.expectation: Digital tools that offer community, vulnerability, and support for those navigating loss.
service_gaps.perception: Apps that feel invasive, obsessed with metrics, and geared toward healthy, baseline pregnancies.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Development of a peer-led digital platform for high-risk/post-loss individuals focusing on emotional check-ins rather than cycle-tracking.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'Trauma-Informed Intake' protocol where providers are prompted to discuss the patient's emotional history before presenting clinical data.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want a provider to look at me—not my chart—and ask, 'How are you really doing?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Mobile app feature for post-loss patients that provides emotional resource navigation rather than just symptom/ovulation tracking.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don’t need an app to tell me when to test—I need one that acknowledges how terrifying it is to wait for the test.

expectations.text: I thought it would be a familiar, comforting routine. I anticipated being the 'patient' in the way I’ve always treated patients: clearly, efficiently, and with the assumption that the protocols would catch anything that went wrong.
I expected—or perhaps I just desperately hoped—for a partnership. I wanted that 'gold standard' continuity where I wasn't just a chart number in the system, but a person with a specific story.

perceptions.text: The environment felt surprisingly intimidating, even though I knew the layout of the clinic better than anyone. I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but I actually felt exposed.
The reality has been more like a revolving door. I see whoever is on the rotation that day... There is zero continuity of care.

valued_items.text: It wasn't a conversation about the pregnancy or the risks, just an acknowledgement that I’m a human being who needs to eat. It was so simple, yet it felt more meaningful than a lot of the well-meaning but empty 'congratulations'

missing_items.text: I wanted someone to stop, look at me, and just say, 'You’re doing a good job, and your baby is okay.'
I didn't want a soft touch; I wanted transparency... It’s hard to feel like you’re being managed rather than informed.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional, data-driven partnership with transparency.
service_gaps.perception: Filtered information and condescending 'practiced smiles' designed to keep a 'geriatric' patient calm.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop 'clinician-patient' pathways for pregnant healthcare workers that allow for raw data access.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent relationship with a single provider.
service_gaps.perception: A 'revolving door' of strangers requiring redundant history taking.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Primary Midwife' model or dedicated clinical concierge for high-risk/complex cases.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Clinical-Peer Support' forum or group specifically for healthcare professionals navigating pregnancy to reduce isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’ve found myself leaning heavily on a small group of former colleagues... I’m looking for a mirror to my own experience.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign triage protocols for patients with medical expertise to bypass initial administrative 'gatekeeping'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The system isn’t built for responsiveness; it’s built for liability and efficiency.

expectations.text: I pictured a seamless, data-driven partnership—a sort of elevated dialogue where we’d discuss placental markers and early fetal development as equals.
I expected a proactive plan, maybe extra touchpoints or just a space to acknowledge that this isn't my first rodeo with grief.

perceptions.text: The reality, though, has been far more isolating. It feels like I’m caught in this strange middle ground... the emotional labor is left entirely to me.
My OB is incredibly efficient, but that efficiency feels like a barrier.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to acknowledge that I was feeling a bit fragile, not just acknowledge my professional credentials.
I need the clinical nuance, but they seem to think that because I’m a professional, I should just be happy with the 'all clear'.

missing_items.text: There’s no one bridging the gap to say, 'Yes, that’s normal, and here is why it’s happening, and here is how you can manage the discomfort.'
There was no psychological support, no 'how are you holding up,' just a business-as-usual approach.

service_gaps.expectation: Anticipated a partnership where knowledge acts as a buffer and clinician provides nuance.
service_gaps.perception: System treats her as a 'colleague' to be managed efficiently, excluding her from emotional care.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a clinical role for 'Patient Educators' who can interpret data and hold space for patients with medical literacy.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care with a single provider who knows her history.
service_gaps.perception: Fractured rotation where she re-explains her story at every visit.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Digital Narrative Charts' that alert providers to patient history before entry.

innovation_opportunities.description: An 'Expert Patient' Care Protocol that bypasses standard 'low-risk' scripts for medical professionals to allow for nuanced data discussions without losing emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to be treated like a colleague in my own care, not a subordinate.

innovation_opportunities.description: A 'Clinical Synthesis' tool that provides context for lab results, explaining not just 'normal' ranges, but the specific clinical 'why' behind the numbers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It’s not just about the information; it’s about the interpretation.

expectations.text: I thought I’d be able to manage the physical side of things just like I manage my shifts at the clinic. I thought I’d be 'fine' because I knew the anatomy and the protocols.
I expected the follow-up visits to be a sort of safety net. I thought that even if I was struggling, a professional would be there to spot it.

perceptions.text: The care I received… it was efficient, I guess. That’s the word they’d use.
I felt like I had to hide the fact that I was struggling or feeling overwhelmed, because how could the 'nurse' not know how to feed her own baby?

valued_items.text: I needed someone to talk to about the anxiety... I needed permission to be a mother instead of a colleague.

missing_items.text: I wish one of them had just sat down, ignored my professional title, and asked me how I was doing, rather than just checking if the baby was fed.

service_gaps.expectation: Health professionals would provide a safety net to spot emotional/mental health struggles.
service_gaps.perception: Appointments were fast, functional, and superficial because providers focused only on clinical metrics and assumed professional competence equalled emotional stability.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing mandatory, non-clinical emotional health 'human check-ins' that are decoupled from clinical vitals.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Human Check-in' protocols for all postpartum mothers that intentionally decouple professional identity from maternal support needs.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would design a system that requires a 'human check-in' for every mother, regardless of their medical background.

innovation_opportunities.description: Peer support or mentor programs for healthcare workers transitioning into motherhood to provide a 'safe space' that avoids clinical judgment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We need a model where, for the first few months, you have access to someone—a mentor, a therapist, or just another mother—who isn't there to check your vitals.

expectations.text: I thought, maybe if I could just get a doctor to really look me in the eye, we could talk about what I was scared of, how I handle pain, and what my limits were.
I wanted to believe it would be a place of care—a sanctuary where I could finally breathe, knowing that help was right there.
I thought, Okay, now that the emergency is over and the babies are home, someone is going to sit me down and walk me through what actually happened.

perceptions.text: The environment was loud, sterile, and blindingly bright. It didn't feel like a space made for bringing life into the world; it felt like an emergency department.
They didn't give me attention; they gave me interventions. They were present in the room, but they weren't with me.
It felt less like a choice and more like a surrender. I wanted so badly to be their source of comfort, but I just felt like I was failing at the one thing I thought I could control.

valued_items.text: I wanted to hear them cry, to have them placed right on my chest—warm, slippery, and real.
I wanted someone to hold my hand and tell me, 'We’ve got this together'

missing_items.text: I didn't get information; I got directives. It was like, 'We’re doing this because the numbers say so,' and that was the end of it.
I needed someone to sit with me while I was terrified of my own body... I needed presence.

service_gaps.expectation: Engaging in a collaborative conversation about pain management and preferences.
service_gaps.perception: Pain management protocols were imposed as urgent, non-negotiable interventions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create visual, low-barrier preference tools for high-risk patients to communicate non-medical needs (lights, touch, music) even during acute interventions.

service_gaps.expectation: Postnatal check-ups serving as a space to process birth trauma and emotional state.
service_gaps.perception: Check-ups were data-collection exercises focused exclusively on clinical metrics and vitals.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Birth Debriefing' protocol as a standard service component, separated from physical health vitals checks.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a patient-led, non-clinical 'Birth Debrief' consultation that is separate from physical recovery check-ups to allow space for processing emotional trauma.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted them to look at the chart with me, explain the pre-eclampsia, and, more importantly, ask how I was doing—not just how my blood pressure was.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create 'Presence-based' postnatal support modules where staff are trained to prioritize emotional 'holding' and validation during feeding attempts, rather than just technical instruction.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to sit with me while I was terrified of my own body... I needed presence.

expectations.text: I imagined sitting with a doctor who would look at me—not at the computer screen—and ask me how I was doing, how I was managing.
I truly believed that when you are pregnant, there is someone who walks the path with you from the start to the end.
I thought that if I wrote my wishes down, it would be like a contract.

perceptions.text: The environment feels... sterile, I guess is the word. Everything is white and bright, and the people are always rushing.
Every time I go to the clinic, it is like a lottery. I never know who I will see.
She didn't speak my language well—but she just held my hand and looked at me... In that one second, I felt like I was a person again.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to explain things in a way that made me feel like I was doing a good job
She just held my hand and looked at me, really looked at me

missing_items.text: I need someone to translate the meaning behind the data.
I wanted someone—a navigator, maybe—whose job it was to bridge the gap between their office and my life.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent relationship with one provider.
service_gaps.perception: Seeing different, rushed staff every visit who don't know her history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement primary midwife model or case-loading care teams.

service_gaps.expectation: Explanations and shared decision-making during interventions.
service_gaps.perception: Medical staff used clinical jargon and forced procedures without explanation.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Visual decision-support tools and mandatory 'pause-and-explain' protocols.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a patient-navigator role for non-English speakers to bridge language and social barriers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I wanted someone—a navigator, maybe—whose job it was to bridge the gap between their office and my life.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'human-first' digital summary of test results that explains what data means for the mother's daily life, not just clinical risk.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They explain the what of the baby, but never the how.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Warm Welcome' check-in process for labour arrival that prioritizes human connection over data entry.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I had to give my name and my insurance information while I was trying to manage a contraction.

expectations.text: I wanted to walk in, show the provider that I’ve already optimized my folic acid intake... and have them say, 'Great work, let’s keep going.' I wanted it to be collaborative, like peers discussing a project.
I expected to feel steady. I’m twenty-eight, I’ve raised two kids, and I work in medicine—I thought I would be the picture of calm, logical preparation.

perceptions.text: It’s a very one-sided performance. When I’m in that room, I don't feel like a partner; I feel like a subject.
My emotional state is less 'prepared' and more 'braced for impact.'

valued_items.text: I crave a professional who would look at me and say, 'I know you know the data, but how are you holding up?'
I need a space where I can be just a person.

missing_items.text: I’m missing a witness—someone who sees the research and the humanity equally.
None of those resources address the reality of being a human in a compromised position. They don’t talk about the power imbalance in the exam room.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaboration as peers sharing a project.
service_gaps.perception: One-sided performance/subject vs. authority figure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement clinician-patient 'alignment' sessions that explicitly validate the patient's existing knowledge base.

service_gaps.expectation: Recognition of mental health burden/anticipatory anxiety.
service_gaps.perception: System ignores fears if the patient is 'low-risk' or has a clean clinical history.
