service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate routine psychosocial screening that accounts for 'expert patients' and systemic stressors (immigration, status, etc.).

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Whole Person' triage protocol that separates clinical risk from psychosocial vulnerability to ensure patients with 'perfect' histories are not overlooked for support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The system doesn't account for the fact that knowing too much is a form of trauma... I just wish there was a way for the clinical world to value the human as much as they value the outcome.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a 'Patient-Navigated Advocacy' digital tool that helps marginalized or high-knowledge patients prepare for clinical consultations to bridge the power imbalance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed a roadmap for navigating the system, not just the biology. I needed to know how to be safe, not just how to be pregnant.

expectations.text: I pictured myself sitting down with someone who would walk me through the biology of what was happening, almost like a high-level briefing on fetal development.
I really hoped for a 'person.' You know, that idealized version of maternity care where you see the same face every single time.
I expected a partner-in-care dynamic, but the reality is that the time is so short.

perceptions.text: The reality was almost entirely digital and hurried. I’d get a notification on the patient portal that my results were ready, and usually, it was just a string of clinical markers.
It’s like a relay race where I’m the baton being passed from one provider to the next. I have to re-explain my anxieties every single time.
My midwife is kind, and she keeps telling me I’m 'low risk,' which I know is good, but it doesn't stop the anxiety.

valued_items.text: I wanted an intellectual bridge to feel more secure.
I want someone to look at my chart, sure, but then I want them to look at me and ask if this plan actually aligns with who I am.

missing_items.text: There was no space to say, 'The results are fine, but I'm still terrified because I can’t feel them move yet.'
I need a partner, or at least a witness.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent provider who knows my history and name.
service_gaps.perception: Rotation of providers; have to re-explain anxiety and history at every visit.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement a 'Primary Midwife' model with team-based coverage for 'low-risk' patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner-in-care dynamic and shared dialogue regarding health.
service_gaps.perception: Passive recipient of a predetermined, hurried protocol.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Co-designed care plans that include patient-defined concerns and preferences, not just clinical markers.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Clinical Bridge' or 'Patient Liaison' to translate raw digital portal data into humanized explanations.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The reality was almost entirely digital and hurried. I’d get a notification on the patient portal that my results were ready, and usually, it was just a string of clinical markers.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Virtual Warm Handoff' system where patients can document their emotional state/concerns in advance so providers are briefed before the appointment.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I have to re-explain my anxieties every single time, or worse, I have to rely on them having read my notes—which they clearly don't have time to do thoroughly.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Concerns Triage Line' staffed by midwives for non-emergency emotional/clinical reassurance to reduce 'waiting game' anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: The system isn't responsive to me; it's only responsive to crises. If you’re not bleeding or in labor, you’re on your own.

expectations.text: I pictured a partnership. I thought if I had a question or a worry—especially being older—there would be someone on the other end of a phone line who would actually listen.
I expected that being pregnant would unlock some kind of safety net, like the system would automatically lean in to catch me.
I imagined a pre-conception consult where a doctor would sit down, look at my history, and lay out a roadmap.

perceptions.text: The reality feels a lot colder. It feels like I’m navigating a bureaucracy rather than care.
The biggest shock was definitely the disconnect between my internal sense of crisis and their total lack of urgency.
It feels like I’m a passenger in my own body instead of the person carrying this life.

valued_items.text: I need someone to tell me that my fear isn’t 'overreacting'—that it’s just the cost of wanting this so badly.
If they would just take two minutes to sit down and say, 'Here’s why we’re looking for this specific thing, and here’s what this result actually means for your health,' I could breathe again.

missing_items.text: I’ve never once had a provider ask, 'How are you handling the anxiety?'
There was no 'triage' for my anxiety. I’ve learned that unless I’m actively bleeding or in extreme pain, I’m not worth their time.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized, proactive roadmap and partnership.
service_gaps.perception: Protocol-driven, reactive care where the patient is a 'line item' in a busy clinic.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Pre-conception and Early Pregnancy Pathways' that assign a dedicated navigator to higher-risk or anxious patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Accessible, comforting expert advice for concerns.
service_gaps.perception: Phone trees, gatekeeping receptionists, and 24-hour delays in communication.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create a specialized digital triage tool or 'Anxiety-Aware' nurse helpline for prompt non-clinical emotional support.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a 'Pregnancy Anxiety' clinical screening and triage protocol that treats fear as a legitimate medical factor, separate from physical risk status.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to tell me that my fear isn’t 'overreacting'—that it’s just the cost of wanting this so badly.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a patient-facing 'Explainable Data' app module that translates clinical jargon (scans/tests) into personalized, plain-language health context based on maternal age.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to know, 'Does this number look okay for my age? Does this scan show what a healthy embryo should look like at this specific week?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Introduce a 'Continuity Navigator' role: a designated midwife or contact who acts as the primary point of communication to avoid repetitive history-taking.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It’s exhausting to have to 're-introduce' my own anxiety every single visit.

expectations.text: I thought recovery would be physical—like, 'Okay, the stitches are healing, the soreness is fading, check.'
I expected it to be natural, like an instinct that would just kick in the moment she arrived.
I thought the maternity care system would be my guide, a steady hand leading me through the medical and emotional chaos.

perceptions.text: The reality hit me like a brick wall. It’s been nothing but a struggle.
The care feels incredibly transactional. It’s all about the data... They have a checklist, and once the boxes are ticked, the visit is over.
My midwife is wonderful, really, but when she’s here, I find myself nodding along and acting like I’ve got it all figured out.

valued_items.text: I need someone to acknowledge that my heart is breaking alongside the physical difficulty... I need someone to hold space for the fact that I feel like a failure.

missing_items.text: No one ever checked in on the woman who was trying to learn how to exist in this new reality.
I’d want a mandatory, non-negotiable 'check-in' for me, where someone sat down and actually asked, 'How are you holding up emotionally?'

service_gaps.expectation: Midwife as a coach/guide for the transition to motherhood.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical, mechanical, and transactional care focusing solely on physical data.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal visits to include a 'whole-person' assessment alongside physical checks.

service_gaps.expectation: Breastfeeding as a natural, calm, bonding instinct.
service_gaps.perception: Frustrating, high-stakes, technical struggle that feels like failing a test.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement peer-led or emotional-support focused breastfeeding support groups that validate the mental toll.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated Mother-Focused Check-in: Add a standardized emotional health component to all pediatric follow-ups.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’d want that support to be integrated into the pediatric visits so I don't feel like I’m 'derailing' the process by talking about my own anxiety.

innovation_opportunities.description: Validation-based Coaching: A service model that prioritizes holding space for identity loss and emotional distress, not just physical recovery.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Just let us be those things without trying to 'fix' it with a pamphlet or a quick, clinical answer.

expectations.text: I figured if I wrote it down, it would act like a shield, something the nurses and doctors would respect as a baseline for my care.
I assumed they’d explain the trade-offs—especially with the pre-eclampsia—and we’d decide together on what would keep me stable.
I wanted a sense of security... I expected a little bit of warmth, or at least a recognition that this was a huge, scary life event for me.

perceptions.text: The staff was focused on the numbers—my blood pressure, the baby’s heart rate—which I get, I do. But they stopped seeing me.
It felt like they just defaulted to the 'standard protocol' for high-risk patients without asking me what I felt I needed.
The reality was clinical and cold. The moment I walked through those double doors, the atmosphere was sterile and hurried.

valued_items.text: I wanted to feel like a partner, not a subject.
I wanted the room to quiet down, the lights to dim, and for them to place my baby on my chest so I could finally breathe.

missing_items.text: They didn't stop. She didn't even look up from the screen... I just wanted her to stop for a second, maybe just take my hand and tell me I was going to be okay.
There was no gentle hand-off, no calm introduction. It was all hurried movements and shouting.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative conversation regarding birth plan and interventions.
service_gaps.perception: Emergency protocols enforced without consultation or acknowledgement of the patient.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementation of a 'Rapid Consultation Protocol' for clinical emergencies that mandates a 30-second pause for patient communication.

service_gaps.expectation: Human-centric warmth and recognition of the emotional weight of birth.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical sterility and erasure of the patient as an individual.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Staff training on 'Person-First' high-risk care models to prevent dehumanization during acute clinical management.

innovation_opportunities.description: Trauma-informed care pathways that include mandatory post-birth debriefs for high-risk patients to bridge the gap between clinical outcomes and emotional recovery.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I expected the follow-ups to be a place of reconciliation—a time where someone would finally sit down and walk me through what happened.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of 'Patient-Facing Data Dashboards' that allow clinicians to explain the 'why' of clinical spikes (BP, etc.) in real-time to maintain patient agency.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I couldn't 'out-think' the situation, and the fact that my medical background was completely ignored by the staff made me feel like my entire professional life was a lie.

expectations.text: I wanted my first appointment to be this grand, collaborative exhale... acknowledging the journey.
I really clung to this fantasy of having a 'champion'—someone who knew my chart inside and out.
I want to be treated as a partner in that decision.

perceptions.text: It felt a bit like being processed at the DMV. It was quick, efficient, and very much focused on the immediate clinical metrics.
The reality, though, has been a rotating door of providers. Every time I walk in, I have to give the 'Bettina Recap'.
This doctor actually looked at my chart... She gave me two extra minutes to talk about how the ghost of those years... still haunts me.

valued_items.text: I wanted to feel like a participant in my own care, someone whose perspective on her own body actually mattered.
I want someone to acknowledge that my history still matters, even when the current medical report is perfect.

missing_items.text: There is no infrastructure for your fear... there’s no protocol for someone who is terrified that her past is waiting to ambush her.
I just wanted someone to talk to, but the system isn't built for conversation; it’s built for emergencies.

service_gaps.expectation: Appointment as a collaborative, personalized exhale acknowledging emotional history.
service_gaps.perception: Mechanical, data-driven 'processing' focused only on clinical milestones.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardize patient-centered 'history intake' forms that follow the patient.

service_gaps.expectation: A consistent, single 'champion' midwife or doctor.
service_gaps.perception: Rotating door of providers requiring repetitive history recaps.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient narrative summary available to all clinical staff.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Patient Story' brief that is summarized and attached to the top of the digital chart for all providers to read before entry.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I spend the first five minutes of every appointment playing catch-up, which is exhausting when you’re already feeling vulnerable.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create 'Trauma-Informed Birth Planning' sessions with dedicated counselors who validate history-based anxieties.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to be treated as a partner in that decision... I’m terrified that if a complication arises, the professional mask will slip.

innovation_opportunities.description: Introduce a 'Mental Health Triage' line for non-emergency, anxiety-driven concerns to provide reassurance without requiring ER visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I just wanted someone to talk to, but the system isn't built for conversation; it’s built for emergencies.

expectations.text: I pictured sitting down with a doctor who would look at my cycles, validate the effort I’m putting in, and maybe say, 'Okay, Corie, if we don't see a change in a couple of months, here’s a plan.'
I imagined myself being this calm, 'go-with-the-flow' mother who would just welcome the process as it came.

perceptions.text: My doctor seems to view my involvement as... I don’t know, obsessive? Like I’m just a young woman who needs to go home, relax, and stop thinking so much.
The apps tell me when I’m ovulating, sure, but they don't help when the biology doesn't align with the screen. They give you the 'when,' but they leave you entirely alone with the 'why.'

valued_items.text: I just want someone to take me seriously.
I needed a bridge to actual medical expertise.

missing_items.text: I need someone—a professional—to look me in the eye and say, 'I see that you’re struggling, and your feelings are valid.'
I expected them to treat the pre-conception phase with as much medical rigor as they do the actual pregnancy.

service_gaps.expectation: Partnering with a doctor to analyze cycles and create a proactive plan.
service_gaps.perception: Doctor dismissing data as 'obsessive' and using 'wait and see' approach.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Standardized preconception intake protocols that incorporate patient-led data.

service_gaps.expectation: Digital tools providing clarity and actionable medical insight.
service_gaps.perception: Tools providing basic/commercial content that exacerbates isolation and confusion.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Clinical integration of fertility tracking data into Electronic Health Records (EHR) for provider review.

innovation_opportunities.description: Preconception 'Data Bridge' portal where patients can upload cycle logs that are pre-reviewed by a nurse navigator before appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed a bridge to actual medical expertise.

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual support groups or peer-mentorship programs for remote/rural mothers to mitigate isolation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m craving a space where I don’t have to justify my desire for another child or defend why I’m so fixated on my health.

innovation_opportunities.description: Decision-support tools for clinicians to handle 'low-risk' proactive patients without defaulting to dismissive 'relax' advice.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It shouldn't be this hard to get someone to care about your reproductive future.

expectations.text: I imagined the doctor’s office to be a place where we sit and talk—not just about numbers, but about the baby.
I expected to feel guided, like someone would hold my hand and tell me, 'This is what you do, and this is how you take care of her.'
I thought that since I am carrying a person, I would be assigned to a person, too... somebody who would learn my face.

perceptions.text: The rooms are very white, very bright. There is a lot of paper. They measure my stomach, they listen to the machine go whoosh-whoosh, and then they give me a list of things to read.
They come in, they check the blood pressure, they check the heartbeat, and then they stand by the door with their hand on the handle. It is like they are already gone.
When they showed me the picture, the doctor actually stopped for a second. She pointed and said, 'Look, that is the heart.'

valued_items.text: For that one minute, the language barrier did not matter. She was looking at my baby, and I was looking at my baby, and we were both smiling.
I write down the words I didn't understand at the clinic so I can look them up slowly, without the pressure of a nurse waiting by the door.

missing_items.text: Nobody ever asks me how I am feeling in my heart, or if I am scared to be alone at night.
I need someone to talk to me, not just give me a printout to take home.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized guidance and mentorship
service_gaps.perception: Mechanical, transactional encounters with rotating providers
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Assign a dedicated midwife/navigator for consistent relationship building.

service_gaps.expectation: Clear, accessible, and empathetic information
service_gaps.perception: Complex medical jargon provided via web portals and rapid verbal instructions
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Multilingual, plain-language patient education tools and visual summaries.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-provider 'Warm Handoff' tool: A digital or physical profile card that shares the patient's emotional state, fears, and personal context to prevent the need for repeating life stories.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I have to start the story from the beginning every time. 'Yes, this is my first baby. Yes, I am 22. Yes, I live in Boston.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Virtual 'Bridge' Consultations: Dedicated 5-minute sessions focused on emotional well-being and Q&A, separated from the clinical check-up to reduce pressure and anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I think if they just sat with me for five minutes—not ten seconds—and let me ask my 'stupid' questions, I would feel much, much safer.

innovation_opportunities.description: Visual/Non-Verbal communication aids in clinic rooms: A menu of common pregnancy terms translated visually to bypass language barriers and reduce the stress of translation during exams.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I am terrified I will drop it because I don’t understand the instructions. Sometimes the nurse speaks so quickly, and I just nod.

expectations.text: I pictured someone you could actually talk to—not just a face behind a clipboard who's checking a box.
I expected a plan. Not just a generic pamphlet, but a conversation.
I thought the emotional weight would be shared, that the 'high risk' part wouldn't be something I was carrying entirely on my own.

perceptions.text: It feels like I’m being managed like a piece of equipment that might break, rather than a person growing a life.
The apps are all sunshine and rainbows... but then I get to the doctor, and the tone is purely clinical, cold, and heavy.
The clinic is crowded, loud, and feels more like a DMV than a place where new life is starting.

valued_items.text: I wanted someone to sit down with me and say, 'Okay, Jacqui, we know your history, here is how we’re going to manage this together'.
I really hoped for a 'person.' You know, someone who would recognize me when I walked in.

missing_items.text: Nobody has ever asked me, 'Jacqui, how are you actually going to get here?' or 'How are you going to manage the stress of all this by yourself?'
They don't tell me what to look for, how to ask my boss for accommodations, or how to calm the panic when I’m alone in my apartment at night.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a supportive partnership and emotional safety net.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'high-risk case' or 'piece of equipment' with cold, transactional interactions.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement trauma-informed care and dedicated patient navigators for high-risk, solo patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected personalized, actionable advice for a high-risk pregnancy.
service_gaps.perception: Received generic clinical data and 'symptom management' that ignored her socioeconomic reality.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop context-aware digital tools that bridge medical advice with social determinants of health (work/transit/finance).

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Social Determinants of Health' (SDOH) intake module where clinicians map out patient transit and work-life constraints to co-design appointment schedules.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Nobody has ever asked me, 'Jacqui, how are you actually going to get here?'

innovation_opportunities.description: Establish a virtual 'Care Concierge' for high-risk patients who lack a partner, providing log-in support for portal data and basic emotional/logistical check-ins.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: If they had just offered one moment of, 'I know this is hard, let’s see what we can do to make it easier for you,' it would have changed everything.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integration of financial/work advocacy support within maternity clinics to help patients secure employer accommodations for mandatory medical appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They don't tell me... how to ask my boss for accommodations.

expectations.text: I had this arrogant, intellectual certainty that because I understood the mechanisms, I would be able to manage the recovery.
I expected the check-ups to be a space of sanctuary... a partnership—someone to hold the medical weight so I could just focus on holding my son.

perceptions.text: The reality? It was a violent intrusion. Nothing I learned in textbooks prepared me for the actual sensation of feeling so fundamentally unmoored.
In practice? It was a cold, efficient transaction. My care team saw my resume—they saw 'nutrition specialist'—and they essentially checked out.

valued_items.text: I wanted a space where I could say, 'I am terrified,' without being offered a lecture on the physiological normality of my anxiety.
We need a model that integrates mental health and trauma-informed support into the very first prenatal appointment and carries it through well into the postpartum period.

missing_items.text: They saw a peer, not a patient... I was utterly starved of human care.
I was managed, measured, and released, but I was never really witnessed.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected care to include psychological holding and emotional support.
service_gaps.perception: Received only clinical data-driven check-ups based on 'low-risk' status.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement routine relational mental health screenings that bypass 'professional' status bias.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement 'Trauma-Informed Relational Rounds' that prioritize asking about the mother's emotional state ('the ghosts') rather than just clinical markers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: We need care that asks about the ghosts, not just the hemoglobin.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a 'Patient-Provider Communication Protocol' that explicitly suspends assumptions based on a patient’s professional background to ensure all receive basic emotional support.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They saw my credentials and assumed I had no need for comfort.

expectations.text: I thought if I laid out my preferences clearly, the staff would look at me and think, 'Okay, this girl knows what she's doing.'
I expected a conversation... you explain the risks, the benefits, and the alternatives, and you let the patient decide what fits their threshold.
I expected to hear him cry, have them immediately place him on my chest—skin-to-skin

perceptions.text: When I handed it over, it felt like the midwife didn't even read it. She just glanced at the paper... It didn't feel like a roadmap... it felt like a piece of paper that made me look like I was trying too hard.
It wasn't a conversation; it was a sales pitch. As soon as the contractions started... she just kept hovering with the epidural talk.
It was a tactical operation. There was no gentle hand-off. He was taken... and immediately whisked away to that stainless-steel warmer.

valued_items.text: I really wanted to try using the tub for labor... I’d be able to manage the physical sensation without needing to be stuck in a bed.
I wanted to feel safe, like I was in capable hands that wouldn't let anything happen to my baby.

missing_items.text: I needed someone to sit with me and acknowledge the emotional weight of it. I didn’t just need someone to fix the latch; I needed someone to say, 'This is incredibly hard...'
I wanted someone to use the word 'trauma' or at least acknowledge that it was a valid reaction.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional collaboration and respect for nursing knowledge.
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'teen mom' who is 'difficult' or 'anxious'; dismissed clinically.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Staff sensitivity training to remove bias based on age/status.

service_gaps.expectation: Informed consent via dialogue.
service_gaps.perception: Unilateral maneuvers and authoritative commands.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Structured communication tools for clinicians to ensure patient involvement.

innovation_opportunities.description: Create a digital, interactive birth preference tool that integrates into the clinical dashboard so staff are required to acknowledge patient input.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I treated it like a clinical care plan... But when I handed it over, it felt like the midwife didn't even read it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'postpartum wellness liaison' who visits patients at home or clinic specifically to discuss the emotional and psychological trauma of the birth event.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to sit with me and acknowledge the emotional weight of it... They didn't see the underlying fear.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a dedicated 'student/young parent' support pathway that pairs them with mentors to bridge the gap between their clinical training and their patient experience.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I feel like I’m not allowed to be a kid who just became a mother. I’m expected to be this perfectly informed, calm, maternal professional.

expectations.text: I expected that here, in a big city like Boston, there would be so much technology, so many resources to help me feel steady. I hoped for someone to sit with me, to explain the things that are different here.

I expected that we would talk about [pain management]—the different ways to breathe, the water, the movement, maybe a bit of medicine if it became too much. I thought, We will discuss this like adults, like a team.
I expected the birth place to feel like a sanctuary... I imagined soft lighting, perhaps someone to hold my hand, and the smell of something other than antiseptic.

perceptions.text: The reality here is very fast. Tick, tick, tick. The clock is always moving... The nurses rushing, their eyes always on their screens... They don't look at my face much.
Each appointment is like meeting a stranger at a bus stop... One provider checks my blood pressure, another looks at the chart... nobody knows me.

The night nurse... stopped, put her hand on my shoulder—very gently—and she just stayed there for a minute... It was the only time in the whole experience where I felt like someone saw that I was a person.

valued_items.text: I need someone to look at me and say, 'Briana, you are doing a good job, and here is what this means for your body.'
She didn't try to 'fix' me with more papers... She just said, 'You are a very strong woman, Mama.' It was a small thing. A tiny thing.

missing_items.text: I need to be able to ask, 'Is this sharp pain normal?' or 'Why am I feeling this heavy?' and have someone answer me with a human voice, not a printed page.
They don't know that I skip meals so the children can eat... They give me advice for a life I do not live.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional collaboration/partnership
service_gaps.perception: Treated as a 'low-risk' case, silenced when attempting to share clinical knowledge
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Provider training to leverage patient clinical literacy

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care/Relationship-based care
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented, revolving door of providers who do not know the patient's story
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implementing primary midwife models or 'passport' digital patient summaries

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-provider 'connection cards' that allow patients to share personal goals, language needs, and social stressors (like transportation/food security) before appointments.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I just want someone to look at me, not the chart, and see the woman behind the mother.

innovation_opportunities.description: Establishment of peer-led immigrant maternity support groups to provide the 'village' network missing in urban centers.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: What I missed most is... someone to help us navigate the paperwork or the bus routes.

innovation_opportunities.description: Cultural humility training for staff specifically addressing the impact of language barriers on patient agency and the silencing of immigrant voices.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Because I struggle with the language, I see how they look at me when I try to describe a symptom. They treat me like I am confused.

expectations.text: I thought if I put in the work... the doctor would see me as an ally in my own health.
I expected the system to be a partner in risk assessment.

perceptions.text: It’s just been… transactional. Every visit feels like a sprint.
I feel like just a number on a Medicaid card.

valued_items.text: I want someone to listen to me, to look me in the eye and take my concerns seriously.
I’m asking for a seat at the table where the decisions about my baby’s life are being made.

missing_items.text: From the medical side, what’s missing is simple human curiosity.
There’s no middle ground for a mother who’s just trying to be careful and practical.

service_gaps.expectation: Partner in risk assessment regarding environmental health.
service_gaps.perception: Met with a blank stare/dismissal.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrate environmental health screening into prenatal assessment protocols.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional collaboration/validation of patient knowledge.
service_gaps.perception: Treated like a 'silly child' or 'nuisance'.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Adopt 'Shared Decision-Making' tools that incorporate patient-researched questions.

innovation_opportunities.description: Patient-provider 'Partnership Roadmap' document to align research/goals before clinical exams.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to walk into that clinic and have someone look at me and say, 'Dwana, you've done the work, we respect that, let's talk about the plan.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Evidence-based, low-income accessible health resource library to bridge clinical data with domestic reality.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There’s no bridge between the clinical, cold data and the real-life struggle of keeping a household running.

innovation_opportunities.description: Inclusion of environmental health social determinants in routine care intake forms.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They don't see the connection between the poverty of my neighborhood and the biology of my body.

expectations.text: I expected to be treated like a colleague, almost—you know, someone who would want the jargon, the stats, and the 'why' behind every test.
I’ll be honest, I expected a phone call. In my old professional life, that’s how we did it—the call, the 'don't panic, but we need to discuss further testing' speech.
I suppose I expected a sense of narrative continuity—the idea that someone would hold the thread of my story from the first positive test to the final weeks.

perceptions.text: It was just an update in the patient portal. You click the link, and suddenly you’re staring at a PDF that tells you if your child’s genetic future looks like a probability curve or a reality.
The reality has been much more fragmented... I’m constantly 're-introducing' myself to the room.
My OB is wonderful, but she caught on early that I was over-analyzing... She just put a hand on my arm and said, 'Chanell, for today, let’s just look at this heartbeat.'

valued_items.text: That question caught me so off-guard I almost cried... It was about my autonomy... She invited me to bridge that gap between the professional who understands the protocols and the mother who needs to feel she has a say.
I want someone who remembers my name and the shape of my story, so I don’t have to feel like I’m starting from zero every single time.

missing_items.text: I don't need a more detailed breakdown... I need someone to bridge the gap between those numbers and the reality of a living, breathing baby.
I don't just want a medical provider; I want someone who remembers my name and the shape of my story.

service_gaps.expectation: Personalized delivery of medical results via discussion.
service_gaps.perception: Automated, depersonalized PDF delivery via patient portal.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Hybrid digital/human notification system for sensitive results.

service_gaps.expectation: Narrative continuity with a known provider.
service_gaps.perception: Fragmented care across rotating providers.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital 'story-so-far' brief for clinicians to review before appointments.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider dashboard that includes a 'Patient Narrative' summary, reducing the need for patients to repeat their history.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want someone who remembers my name and the shape of my story, so I don’t have to feel like I’m starting from zero.

innovation_opportunities.description: Redesign of abnormal result notification protocol to include an immediate option to schedule a 'results consultation' call rather than silent PDF release.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: It was just an update in the patient portal... There’s no 'who' in that moment.

expectations.text: I imagined it’d be a partnership where I could say, 'I’m scared I’m doing this too young,' or 'I’m drowning in the chaos at home,' and someone would help me navigate the transition.
I thought there’d be forums or articles that got the nuance of having three toddlers already running around while trying to manage a pregnancy.

perceptions.text: It’s clinical. The support is focused, but it’s narrow. It’s all about the blood pressure, the weight, the heartbeat, and the vitamins.
The staff is professional, but it’s assembly-line work. You go in, you pee in a cup, you get weighed, you get your blood drawn.

valued_items.text: My midwife is great—she doesn't treat me like a kid who needs a pamphlet on how to raise a baby.

missing_items.text: I need someone to look at me and acknowledge that I’m exhausted... I’m not just a container for a baby; I’m a human being.
There’s no one asking if I can actually make it to all these appointments, or how I’m managing childcare.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a partnership to navigate the transition and mental load.
service_gaps.perception: Care is strictly focused on clinical data (vitals, weight, heartbeat).
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'whole-person' intake assessments that track life-load/social determinants alongside clinical metrics.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected consistent care provider to build on past history.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of providers; must constantly re-explain history.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Digital patient narrative summary that providers must review prior to appointments.

innovation_opportunities.description: Life-stage specific information portals that address the mental load of multiparous, young, or non-traditional families rather than generic medical pamphlets.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I needed someone to talk about the mental exhaustion of motherhood or how to hold it together when you're overwhelmed.

innovation_opportunities.description: Standardized 'Human Check-in' protocol for providers to use during clinical visits.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They check the box for 'prenatal care,' but they don't provide any 'life care.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Resource coordination tool to help connect mothers with childcare or practical support for appointment attendance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There’s no one asking if I can actually make it to all these appointments, or how I’m managing childcare.

expectations.text: I thought I’d be dealing with healing stitches and sleep deprivation, just the usual 'new mom' territory.
I really wanted this to be that one quiet, natural bridge between me and my baby.
I expected the check-ups to be a place where someone would actually help me put the pieces back together.

perceptions.text: Every appointment was about my viral load or the vaccines for the baby. I felt like I had to be the one to remind everyone.
The visits were fast, transactional, and focused entirely on the numbers... I left every single appointment feeling smaller and more alone.
They saved my life, but they didn't help me live it afterwards.

valued_items.text: I wanted to be seen. I wanted someone to look at me—not just the chart—and ask how my brain was handling what I’d gone through.

missing_items.text: I didn't have anyone nearby to hold the other kid so I could actually focus.
They didn't give me the tools to actually feel safe in it [my body].

service_gaps.expectation: Expected postnatal check-ups to be a place for holistic recovery and processing trauma.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced fast, transactional appointments focused only on vitals/biometrics.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign postnatal visits to include a mandatory 'whole-person' mental health assessment component.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected breastfeeding to be a quiet, bonding, and controlled experience.
service_gaps.perception: Experienced it as a source of panic due to physical anxiety and lack of support.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Integrated lactation support that includes trauma-informed care and remote access for rural patients.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postpartum Liaison program: A dedicated professional to follow high-risk patients for the first 3 months post-discharge.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I would make it mandatory—not optional... to have a dedicated postpartum liaison who stays with you for at least the first three months.

innovation_opportunities.description: Remote vital/mental health check-in tools that focus on patient-reported outcomes rather than just vitals.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m still checking my blood pressure... staring at those numbers like they’re going to tell me if I’m safe or not.

expectations.text: I thought having it all written down would make me feel in control... they’ll respect my wishes, they’ll see me.
I expected a conversation, a real dialogue where my midwives would help me navigate the pain based on how I was coping in the moment.
I imagined a warm, almost ritualistic space where people would acknowledge that this was my first—and likely only—chance at this.

perceptions.text: The medical team was polite, sure, but I felt like they were constantly looking at me through the lens of my age, not my preferences.

She reached for a warm blanket, tucked it around my shoulders, and just rested her hand on my arm for a second. She didn’t say anything medical. She just whispered, 'I know you're doing the hardest work there is.'
It felt like I was being processed through a machine that had no space for the human element.

valued_items.text: I wanted to be treated like an intelligent partner in my own medical care.
I needed someone to sit with me and tell me that it was okay if I was terrified.

missing_items.text: I needed a witness to my struggle... someone to say, 'You are doing enough.'
There was no discussion about whether we could monitor me in the water... It was just a hard 'no.'

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, informed decision-making based on personal preferences.
service_gaps.perception: Instructions given as directives without room for dialogue or autonomy.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement decision-support tools that bridge patient preferences with clinical realities.

service_gaps.expectation: Warm, holistic, and ritualistic birth environment.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical, sterile atmosphere focused on equipment and checklists.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create 'Sanctuary-First' protocols for labor rooms to minimize medical intrusiveness.

innovation_opportunities.description: Integrated logistical support service for expectant parents without personal transport, reducing anxiety about birth timing.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I don't even own a car. Every time I have a Braxton Hicks contraction... I start calculating the cost and the reliability of a ride-share.

innovation_opportunities.description: Postnatal mental health 'debrief' sessions that move beyond clinical check-lists to include psychological validation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: They gave me a survey—one of those standard 'on a scale of one to ten' depression screenings—and because I didn't hit the absolute 'red flag' threshold... they basically shrugged.

innovation_opportunities.description: Digital platform for 'Care Coordination' to connect isolated parents with support groups or local resources.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: There’s no village to turn to, no one to hand the baby to... The gap wasn't just wide—it was an abyss.

expectations.text: I expected to be treated like a liability—a 'geriatric pregnancy' in the sterile, cold sense of the term.
I expected a very structured, somewhat detached flow of information. You know how it goes: the technician stays quiet during the scan.
I fully expected to be shuffled between residents, fellows, and nurses, spending half my time repeating my history.

perceptions.text: My OB office feels less like a sterile lab and more like a space where they actually prioritize the person.
My doctor is so communicative that she talks through the ultrasound in real-time... But being a professional, I know that even a clear scan is just a snapshot in time.
The practice I’m with has been remarkably consistent. I see my primary OB at almost every visit.

valued_items.text: We talk through the data, and she listens when I voice my concerns about the 'medium risk' label hanging over me.
Finding a few women who have walked this path at forty was a revelation. They don’t want to talk about my blood pressure or my risk factors—they want to talk about the sheer, overwhelming exhaustion.

missing_items.text: What I find myself craving—and what feels impossible to ask for—is for someone to hold the space for my fear without pathologizing it.
My OB provides excellent clinical continuity, but I’m not sure anyone is providing emotional continuity.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected a detached medical environment focused on risk assessment.
service_gaps.perception: Received professional transparency and high clinical competence, but environment remains fundamentally designed to watch for failure.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create 'Sanctuary Spaces' within clinical settings that minimize visual/auditory markers of surveillance.

service_gaps.expectation: Expected data to provide control and comfort.
service_gaps.perception: Data provided awareness of helplessness and triggers hyper-vigilance.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Shift information delivery to include 'emotional processing' components rather than just raw data.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider-led 'Emotional Check-in' protocol to decouple clinical status from psychological state.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want someone to hold the space for my fear without pathologizing it.

innovation_opportunities.description: Mentorship program connecting older pregnant patients for peer-validation.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Finding a few women who have walked this path at forty was a revelation.

innovation_opportunities.description: Pre-birth 'Care Partnership' template that allows clinicians to acknowledge a patient's professional background while formalizing 'permission to be a patient'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I’m starving for someone to just sit with the fear, to let me be fragile for a moment without trying to 'fix' it.

expectations.text: I envisioned sitting down with an OB-GYN who would look at my charts, see that I understand the mechanics of early development, and meet me on that level.
I expected a peer-to-peer discussion where I could voice my anxieties about trophoblast differentiation and have them be validated.

perceptions.text: The reality has been… patronizing. When I walk into a clinic, they see an eighteen-year-old girl and they shift into this weird, gentle, almost condescending tone.
The provider didn't even look at me as a person with agency. She spent the first ten minutes asking about my home life... almost like she was auditing my suitability to be a mother.

valued_items.text: I need a provider who can say, 'Elenore, I understand your concern about this specific statistic, so let's look at the data together so you can breathe.'
I’m looking for tools that provided data-backed insights, maybe some interactive models of what’s happening at a cellular level.

missing_items.text: The biggest void is definitely the lack of intellectual space. From the professionals, I’m missing a provider who sees my medical literacy as an asset.
There is no space for a user who wants to track the probability of implantation based on specific hormonal fluctuations.

service_gaps.expectation: Professional partnership and peer-to-peer clinical discussion.
service_gaps.perception: Patronizing gatekeeping of information and dismissal of technical inquiries.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Create clinician protocols for 'high-health-literacy' patients.

service_gaps.expectation: Digital tools offering data-backed, granular biometrics.
service_gaps.perception: Patronizing apps with soft pastels and simplified, infantalizing content.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop 'Advanced Mode' toggle in maternity apps for clinical-level data/research.

innovation_opportunities.description: Provider patient-segmentation tool to identify high-health-literacy patients who prefer technical/data-driven communication.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need a provider who can say, 'Elenore, I understand your concern about this specific statistic, so let's look at the data together.'

innovation_opportunities.description: Data-centric fertility/preconception app interface with 'Advanced' settings for tracking cellular-level indicators and accessing research journals.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I was looking for tools that provided data-backed insights, maybe some interactive models of what’s happening at a cellular level.

expectations.text: I imagined the clinic would be a place of... tranquilidad. Where I come from, the midwife is like an auntie. You go, she holds your hand, she touches your belly, and she asks about your dreams for the baby.
I really thought—I truly hoped—that I would have one person. Just one. Someone I could look at and see a familiar face, someone who would remember my name.
I expected a conversation... where the doctor would look me in the eye and say, 'Clara, the baby is strong, she is growing just as she should.'

perceptions.text: The reality... it is just bright lights and clicking keyboards. Everything is very white and very loud. They put these cold sensors on me, and they look at the screen more than they look at my face.
It is a revolving door. Every time I go back to that office in Beverly, it is a new face. A new nurse, a new midwife, or sometimes a doctor I have never seen before.
The ultrasound tech, she didn't speak much Spanish, but she saw me tremble when the screen turned on. She reached out and touched my shoulder for just a second. That touch? It felt like home.

valued_items.text: I need someone to say, 'Clara, you are doing a good job, and your baby is strong because you are strong.'
I want to be the one who whispers to her in Spanish, so she knows her roots even if we are a long way from home.

missing_items.text: Everything is missing, in a way. The biggest thing is humanity. They give me information, yes, but it is just information for my brain, not my heart.
I need a person. I don't need another pamphlet.

service_gaps.expectation: Midwife as an auntie figure providing emotional warmth and connection.
service_gaps.perception: Clinical, mechanical, and detached delivery focused on data points.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Person-Centered' care training and social history intake templates.

service_gaps.expectation: Consistent, personalized care from a single provider.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of strangers necessitating repetitive story-telling.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Redesign scheduling to allow for primary midwife assignment (caseload model).

innovation_opportunities.description: Implement a 'Cultural Bridging' program pairing patients with bilingual community doulas to translate medical terms into emotional reassurance.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I want to tell them, 'I know about the gold milk, the colostrum, I know the baby needs me to be calm,' but the words, they get stuck in my throat.

innovation_opportunities.description: Develop a digital 'Personal Story' card in the EHR so every new provider sees a summary of the patient's emotional history/needs before entering the room.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I have to tell my story over and over, like a broken record.

innovation_opportunities.description: Community-based support groups for migrant mothers focused on sharing traditions and emotional validation to replace the missing 'village'.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: Sometimes, I find these little forums for migrant mothers... They understand that I am not just a body.

expectations.text: I think I expected it to be a partnership—like how I manage my fitness clients. You assess the baseline, you set goals, you track the progress, and you pivot when something isn't working.
I expected to find clear, evidence-based systems—sort of like a playbook.
I genuinely expected to have a 'point person.' You know, someone who would recognize my face when I walked in.

perceptions.text: It’s been more like being a passenger on a train I don’t know how to stop. It’s hurried, and it’s very 'one-size-fits-all.'
Most apps and websites are designed with this saccharine, overly bubbly tone that feels like it’s talking down to me.
The reality has been a revolving door. I don’t think I’ve seen the same face twice... Every time I walk into that exam room, I feel like I’m starting from zero.

valued_items.text: I just want to be heard as a person, not a chart.
I’m looking for the mechanism. When I see a result, I want to know the physiology behind it.

missing_items.text: I need someone to acknowledge that being young and unmarried in this system is its own kind of heavy.
What’s missing is a bridge... I get 'bubbly, unrealistic joy' from my phone and 'clinical, detached data' from the doctor. Neither one addresses the fact that I’m a real person.

service_gaps.expectation: Collaborative, evidence-based partnership regarding personal health baseline.
service_gaps.perception: Dismissive 'one-size-fits-all' clinical protocol and lack of curiosity.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Implement 'Person-Centered Data Integration' where patient fitness/health metrics are reviewed as part of the clinical record.

service_gaps.expectation: Continuity of care with a named provider who knows history/context.
service_gaps.perception: Revolving door of staff requiring constant re-explanation and re-auditioning.
service_gaps.innovation_potential: Develop a 'Patient Navigator' or primary midwife model for all maternity patients, regardless of risk status.

innovation_opportunities.description: Evidence-based, high-fidelity digital health platform that avoids 'fluff' and provides clinical, physiological insights.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I didn't need a cartoonish 'pregnancy bestie' app; I needed a clinical, grounded resource that respected my intelligence.

innovation_opportunities.description: Structured 'Emotional Health Debriefs' that move beyond physical vitals to address life-transition anxiety.
innovation_opportunities.source_quote: I need someone to actually sit with me and acknowledge that the loss of control is terrifying.
